description: Read, write, and analyse files — the foundation of every forensic investigation

explanation_styles.analogy: Files are like filing cabinets in an evidence room. open() = pull out the drawer. read() = look at the documents. write() = add new documents. close() = push the drawer back. The with statement is like having an assistant who ALWAYS closes the drawer when you walk away — even if you forget!
explanation_styles.visual: Picture a stream: data flows FROM the file INTO your program (reading) or FROM your program INTO the file (writing). The file object is the pipe connecting them. Mode ('r', 'w', 'a', 'rb') controls the direction and format of the flow.
explanation_styles.step_by_step: Step 1: with open('file.txt', 'r') as f: — opens file, assigns to f. Step 2: content = f.read() — reads entire file into a string. Step 3: Process the content (split, search, analyse). Step 4: with block ends — file automatically closed. Step 5: Use results!
explanation_styles.code_trace: with open('log.txt', 'r') as f: — Python (1) finds the file, (2) opens it in read mode, (3) creates a file object, (4) assigns it to f. f.read() — Python reads bytes from disk, decodes to string. Block ends — Python calls f.close() automatically.
explanation_styles.comparison: Java: BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(new FileReader('file.txt')); try { ... } finally { br.close(); }. Python: with open('file.txt') as f: content = f.read(). Python is dramatically simpler and safer!

lessons.title: Files & I/O — Reading, Writing, and Analysing Evidence

theory.introduction:
Every forensic investigation starts and ends with FILES. Log files record what happened. Disk images capture entire hard drives. Memory dumps freeze a computer's state. Network captures store packet data. Configuration files reveal system settings. Without the ability to read, write, and analyse files, you simply cannot do digital forensics.

In the previous topics, you worked with data created inside your scripts — strings, numbers, lists. Now you learn to work with EXTERNAL data: files on disk, evidence from other systems, logs from servers, and reports you generate for your team or for court.

Think of file I/O as the bridge between your Python skills and the real world. Every forensic tool you will ever use — Autopsy, Volatility, Wireshark, Sleuth Kit — reads and writes files. Autopsy reads disk images. Volatility reads memory dumps. Wireshark reads packet captures. Your Python scripts will do the same.

This topic covers: reading text files (logs, configs), writing reports, working with CSV data (structured evidence), binary file analysis (executables, disk images), file hashing for integrity verification, JSON parsing, directory scanning, and error handling for real-world messiness.

VM TIP: On your Kali Linux VM, try: cat /var/log/auth.log | head -20 — that reads the first 20 lines of the authentication log. By the end of this topic, you will build Python tools that do the same thing — and much more.
theory.why_important: File I/O is the #1 most essential skill in forensic programming. Without it, your scripts are isolated from real evidence. With it, you can: parse gigabytes of logs in seconds, verify evidence integrity with hash comparison, extract hidden data from binary files, generate court-ready reports automatically, scan entire directory trees for suspicious files, and process CSV exports from security tools. Every penetration testing report, every incident response timeline, every malware analysis writeup involves reading input files and writing output reports. This is where your Python skills become real forensic capabilities.

key_concepts.explanation:
The with statement is the SAFE way to open files in Python. It guarantees the file is closed when you are done, even if an error occurs. Think of it like an evidence bag that automatically reseals itself — you never accidentally leave evidence exposed.

with open('filename', 'mode') as variable: — this opens the file, assigns it to the variable, runs your code block, then AUTOMATICALLY closes the file. Without with, you must remember to call file.close() manually — and forgetting causes resource leaks.

ALWAYS use with for file operations. It is shorter, safer, and more Pythonic.
key_concepts.example:
# Safe file opening with 'with'
with open('evidence.txt', 'r') as f:
    content = f.read()
    print(content)
# File is automatically closed here!

# WITHOUT with (old, unsafe way):
# f = open('evidence.txt', 'r')
# content = f.read()
# f.close()  # Must remember this!
key_concepts.ai_hint: ALWAYS use with open() as f: — it is like an evidence bag that seals itself. Never worry about forgetting to close files again!

key_concepts.concept: File Modes — Read, Write, Append, Binary
key_concepts.explanation:
File modes control HOW you interact with the file:

'r' = Read (default) — read text. File must exist.
'w' = Write — create new file or OVERWRITE existing. DANGER: destroys old content!
'a' = Append — add to end of file. Safe: does not destroy existing content.
'x' = Exclusive create — create new file, FAIL if it already exists. Safe for evidence.
'rb' = Read binary — read raw bytes (for images, executables, disk images).
'wb' = Write binary — write raw bytes.

In forensics: use 'r' for logs and configs, 'rb' for disk images and executables, 'w' for new reports, 'a' for adding to existing logs. NEVER use 'w' on evidence files — it destroys the original data!
key_concepts.example:
# File modes
# 'r'  — Read text (default)
# 'w'  — Write text (OVERWRITES!)
# 'a'  — Append text (adds to end)
# 'x'  — Create new (fails if exists)
# 'rb' — Read binary (raw bytes)
# 'wb' — Write binary

# Read a text file
with open('log.txt', 'r') as f:
    content = f.read()

# Write a new report
with open('report.txt', 'w') as f:
    f.write('Forensic Report\n')

# Append to a log
with open('audit.log', 'a') as f:
    f.write('New entry\n')

# Read binary (images, executables)
with open('evidence.img', 'rb') as f:
    header = f.read(16)  # First 16 bytes
key_concepts.ai_hint: 'r' = read, 'w' = write (OVERWRITES!), 'a' = append (safe), 'rb' = read binary. NEVER 'w' on evidence — it destroys the original!

key_concepts.explanation:
Python offers several ways to read files, each suited to different situations:

f.read() — reads the ENTIRE file as one string. Good for small files.
f.readline() — reads ONE line at a time. Good for very large files.
f.readlines() — reads ALL lines into a list. Good when you need line numbers.
for line in f: — iterates line by line. BEST for large files (memory efficient).

For forensic log analysis, the for loop pattern is usually best: it handles gigabyte log files without loading everything into memory. Each line is processed and discarded.
key_concepts.example:
# Reading methods
with open('log.txt', 'r') as f:
    # Method 1: Read everything
    all_text = f.read()

with open('log.txt', 'r') as f:
    # Method 2: Read all lines into list
    lines = f.readlines()
    print(f"Total lines: {len(lines)}")

with open('log.txt', 'r') as f:
    # Method 3: Iterate (BEST for large files)
    for line_num, line in enumerate(f, 1):
        if 'FAILED' in line:
            print(f"Line {line_num}: {line.strip()}")
key_concepts.ai_hint: For small files: f.read(). For line-by-line processing (logs, CSVs): for line in f. The for loop is memory-efficient — essential for large evidence files!

key_concepts.explanation:
Writing files creates new content: reports, analysis results, filtered logs, extracted data. Use 'w' mode for new files and 'a' mode to add to existing files.

f.write(string) — writes a string. Does NOT add a newline automatically (you must include \n).
f.writelines(list) — writes a list of strings. Also no automatic newlines.
print(text, file=f) — redirects print to a file. DOES add newline automatically.

CRITICAL: 'w' mode DESTROYS existing content! Always double-check you are not overwriting evidence. Use 'x' mode if you want to fail safely when a file already exists.
key_concepts.example:
# Writing a forensic report
with open('report.txt', 'w') as f:
    f.write('FORENSIC ANALYSIS REPORT\n')
    f.write('=' * 40 + '\n')
    f.write('Date: 2025-02-28\n')
    f.write('Analyst: Synapse Student\n\n')
    
    # Using print with file parameter
    print('Findings:', file=f)
    print('  1. Suspicious login attempts detected', file=f)
    print('  2. Unauthorised file access logged', file=f)

# Verify by reading back
with open('report.txt', 'r') as f:
    print(f.read())
key_concepts.ai_hint: f.write() does NOT add newlines — include \n yourself. print(text, file=f) adds newlines automatically. NEVER use 'w' on evidence files!

key_concepts.concept: File Paths — os.path for Cross-Platform Safety
key_concepts.explanation:
File paths differ across operating systems: Windows uses backslashes (C:\Users\file.txt), Linux/Mac uses forward slashes (/home/user/file.txt). The os.path module handles this automatically.

os.path.join('dir', 'file.txt') — builds paths correctly for ANY operating system.
os.path.exists(path) — checks if file/directory exists before opening.
os.path.basename(path) — gets just the filename from a full path.
os.path.splitext(path) — separates filename from extension.
os.path.getsize(path) — gets file size in bytes.

NEVER hardcode paths with slashes! Always use os.path.join() for portable forensic tools.
key_concepts.example:
import os

# Build paths safely
evidence_dir = os.path.join('cases', 'case_001', 'evidence')
file_path = os.path.join(evidence_dir, 'photo.jpg')
print(f"Path: {file_path}")

# Analyse path components
print(f"Dir:  {os.path.dirname(file_path)}")
print(f"Name: {os.path.basename(file_path)}")
print(f"Ext:  {os.path.splitext(file_path)[1]}")

# Check existence before opening
if os.path.exists(file_path):
    print(f"Size: {os.path.getsize(file_path)} bytes")
else:
    print("File not found!")
key_concepts.ai_hint: os.path.join() builds paths that work on Windows, Linux, AND Mac. os.path.exists() prevents FileNotFoundError. Always check before opening!

key_concepts.explanation:
CSV (Comma-Separated Values) files are everywhere in forensics: firewall logs, IDS alert exports, vulnerability scan results, user activity reports. Python's csv module reads and writes them safely.

csv.reader(f) — reads rows as lists. csv.DictReader(f) — reads rows as dictionaries (column names as keys). csv.writer(f) — writes rows from lists. csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames) — writes from dictionaries.

DictReader is usually better for forensics because you access columns by NAME (row['ip_address']) instead of INDEX (row[3]) — much more readable and less error-prone.
key_concepts.example:
import csv
from io import StringIO

# Read CSV data
csv_data = 'ip,port,status\n10.0.0.1,80,open\n10.0.0.2,443,closed\n'

reader = csv.DictReader(StringIO(csv_data))
for row in reader:
    print(f"{row['ip']}:{row['port']} = {row['status']}")

# Write CSV
with open('scan_results.csv', 'w', newline='') as f:
    writer = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=['ip', 'port', 'status'])
    writer.writeheader()
    writer.writerow({'ip': '10.0.0.1', 'port': 80, 'status': 'open'})
key_concepts.ai_hint: DictReader is best for forensics — access columns by name, not number. Always use newline='' when writing CSV files to prevent double spacing!

key_concepts.explanation:
Binary mode ('rb') reads files as raw bytes, not text. Essential for: disk images (.img, .dd), executables (.exe), memory dumps (.raw), packet captures (.pcap), images (.jpg, .png).

In binary mode, you work with bytes objects (b'\x89PNG'). Key operations: f.read(n) reads n bytes. Bytes can be compared to known signatures (magic numbers) to identify file types regardless of extension.

Magic bytes examples: JPEG starts with FF D8 FF. PNG starts with 89 50 4E 47. PDF starts with 25 50 44 46. EXE starts with 4D 5A (MZ). These signatures cannot be faked by simply renaming a file!
key_concepts.ai_hint: Binary mode reads raw bytes — not text! Magic bytes identify file types by their CONTENT, not their extension. A renamed .exe is still detected as an executable!

key_concepts.explanation:
File hashing is THE most critical forensic skill. When you seize evidence, you hash it immediately. Before court, you hash it again. If hashes match, the evidence was not tampered with. If they differ, the evidence is compromised.

For large files, you cannot read the entire file into memory. Instead, read in CHUNKS: read 4096 bytes, update the hash, read next 4096 bytes, update again, until end of file. This handles files of ANY size — even terabyte disk images.

Standard forensic hashes: MD5 (fast, legacy), SHA-1 (deprecated), SHA-256 (recommended).
key_concepts.example:
import hashlib

def hash_file(filepath, algorithm='sha256', chunk_size=4096):
    """Hash a file of any size using chunked reading."""
    h = hashlib.new(algorithm)
    with open(filepath, 'rb') as f:
        while True:
            chunk = f.read(chunk_size)
            if not chunk:
                break
            h.update(chunk)
    return h.hexdigest()

# Usage:
# sha = hash_file('/evidence/disk.img')
# print(f'SHA-256: {sha}')
key_concepts.ai_hint: Read in chunks (4096 bytes at a time) for ANY file size. Hash verification is legally required for digital evidence admissibility!

key_concepts.explanation:
JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is the standard data format for APIs, configuration files, and modern log systems. Python's json module converts between JSON strings and Python objects.

json.load(file_object) — reads JSON from a file. json.loads(string) — reads JSON from a string. json.dump(data, file_object) — writes Python data to a JSON file. json.dumps(data) — converts to string.

In forensics, JSON appears in: API responses, Elasticsearch logs, cloud service configs, web application data, and many security tool outputs.
key_concepts.example:
import json

# Read JSON
with open('config.json', 'r') as f:
    config = json.load(f)
print(config['server'])

# Write JSON
findings = {'case': 'FR-001', 'threats': ['malware.exe'], 'score': 8.5}
with open('findings.json', 'w') as f:
    json.dump(findings, f, indent=2)

# Pretty print
print(json.dumps(findings, indent=2))
key_concepts.ai_hint: json.load(file) reads from file. json.loads(string) reads from string. The 's' = string! Use indent=2 for readable output.

key_concepts.explanation:
Forensic investigations often require scanning entire directory trees — finding all images in an evidence folder, listing all executables, or cataloguing every file with its hash.

os.listdir(path) — lists items in ONE directory (not recursive).
os.walk(path) — walks through EVERY subdirectory recursively. Returns (dirpath, dirnames, filenames) for each directory it visits.

os.walk() is your recursive forensic scanner. Combined with file hashing and classification, it becomes a complete evidence cataloguing tool.
key_concepts.ai_hint: os.listdir() = one folder. os.walk() = entire tree recursively. os.walk() is your forensic file scanner — it finds EVERYTHING!

key_concepts.concept: Encoding — Handling Text from Different Systems
key_concepts.explanation:
Text files store characters as bytes using an ENCODING. UTF-8 is the modern standard, but evidence from older or international systems may use Latin-1, Windows-1252, or other encodings.

If you open a file with the wrong encoding, you get UnicodeDecodeError or garbled text. Common fix: try UTF-8 first, then Latin-1 (which accepts ANY byte value).

open('file.txt', 'r', encoding='utf-8') — specify encoding explicitly. errors='replace' replaces undecodable bytes with a placeholder instead of crashing.
key_concepts.ai_hint: UTF-8 first, Latin-1 as fallback. errors='replace' prevents crashes on bad bytes. Evidence comes from all kinds of systems — be ready for anything!

key_concepts.explanation:
Files in the real world are messy: they might not exist, you might not have permission, they might be corrupted, or another program might have them locked. Professional forensic tools handle ALL these cases gracefully.

FileNotFoundError — file does not exist at that path.
PermissionError — no read/write permission.
IsADirectoryError — tried to open a directory as a file.
UnicodeDecodeError — wrong text encoding.
OSError — general operating system error (disk full, etc.).

Wrap file operations in try/except to catch these. A good forensic tool logs errors and continues processing the remaining files.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Always try/except around file operations. Return (data, error) tuples so callers can check for problems. A forensic tool that crashes on one bad file is useless!

complete_examples.title: Example 1: Reading and Analysing a Log File
complete_examples.description: Open a text file, read line by line, count threats
complete_examples.code:
# Create a sample log file first
log_content = """2025-02-28 08:15:22 INFO Server started
2025-02-28 08:20:01 WARNING Login FAILED user=admin ip=203.0.113.42
2025-02-28 08:20:02 WARNING Login FAILED user=admin ip=203.0.113.42
2025-02-28 08:20:03 WARNING Login FAILED user=admin ip=203.0.113.42
2025-02-28 08:30:00 INFO Backup completed successfully
2025-02-28 09:00:00 ERROR Connection DENIED from 198.51.100.1
2025-02-28 09:15:00 INFO User john logged in from 10.0.0.5
2025-02-28 10:00:00 CRITICAL Possible brute force ATTACK detected
2025-02-28 11:00:00 INFO Health check passed
2025-02-28 12:00:00 WARNING Disk usage at 85%
"""

# Write sample file
with open('server.log', 'w') as f:
    f.write(log_content)

# Now analyse it!
print("=== LOG FILE ANALYSIS ===")
total_lines = 0
threat_lines = []
level_counts = {}

with open('server.log', 'r') as f:
    for line_num, line in enumerate(f, 1):
        line = line.strip()
        if not line:
            continue
        total_lines += 1
        
        # Extract log level
        parts = line.split()
        if len(parts) >= 3:
            level = parts[2]
            level_counts[level] = level_counts.get(level, 0) + 1
        
        # Check for threats
        if any(word in line.upper() for word in ['FAILED', 'DENIED', 'ATTACK']):
            threat_lines.append((line_num, line))

print(f"Total entries: {total_lines}")
print(f"Threats found: {len(threat_lines)}")
print(f"\nBy level:")
for level, count in sorted(level_counts.items()):
    bar = '#' * (count * 3)
    print(f"  {level:<10} {count} {bar}")
print(f"\nThreat details:")
for num, line in threat_lines:
    print(f"  Line {num}: {line}")
complete_examples.output:
=== LOG FILE ANALYSIS ===
Total entries: 10
Threats found: 5

By level:
  CRITICAL   1 ###
  ERROR      1 ###
  INFO       4 ############
  WARNING    4 ############

Threat details:
  Line 2: 2025-02-28 08:20:01 WARNING Login FAILED user=admin ip=203.0.113.42
  Line 3: ...
  Line 4: ...
  Line 6: 2025-02-28 09:00:00 ERROR Connection DENIED from 198.51.100.1
  Line 8: 2025-02-28 10:00:00 CRITICAL Possible brute force ATTACK detected
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
This is a REAL log analysis pattern:
1. Create a sample log (in real work, the log already exists).
2. open('server.log', 'r') opens in read mode.
3. enumerate(f, 1) gives line numbers starting at 1.
4. .strip() removes leading/trailing whitespace and newlines.
5. .split() breaks the line into parts for level extraction.
6. Keyword search finds threat-related entries.
7. Statistics are collected in dictionaries.

This is exactly how tools like grep, Fail2ban, and OSSEC work — read logs line by line, match patterns, and report findings. On Kali, try: grep -c 'FAILED' /var/log/auth.log
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add IP address extraction: parse the 'ip=' field from failed login lines and count failures per IP.

complete_examples.title: Example 2: Writing a Forensic Report
complete_examples.description: Generate a structured report file from analysis results
complete_examples.code:
import datetime

def generate_report(findings, analyst='Synapse Student', case_id='FR-2025-001'):
    """Generate a formatted forensic report."""
    timestamp = datetime.datetime.now().strftime('%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S')
    
    report_name = f"report_{case_id}.txt"
    with open(report_name, 'w') as f:
        # Header
        f.write('=' * 55 + '\n')
        f.write(f'  FORENSIC ANALYSIS REPORT\n')
        f.write('=' * 55 + '\n')
        f.write(f'  Case ID:    {case_id}\n')
        f.write(f'  Analyst:    {analyst}\n')
        f.write(f'  Generated:  {timestamp}\n')
        f.write('=' * 55 + '\n\n')
        
        # Summary
        f.write('EXECUTIVE SUMMARY\n')
        f.write('-' * 40 + '\n')
        f.write(f'Total findings: {len(findings)}\n')
        critical = sum(1 for item in findings if item["severity"] == "CRITICAL")
        f.write(f'Critical:       {critical}\n\n')
        
        # Detailed findings
        f.write('DETAILED FINDINGS\n')
        f.write('-' * 40 + '\n')
        for i, finding in enumerate(findings, 1):
            f.write(f'\n[{i}] {finding["title"]}\n')
            f.write(f'    Severity:    {finding["severity"]}\n')
            f.write(f'    Evidence:    {finding["evidence"]}\n')
            f.write(f'    Action:      {finding["action"]}\n')
        
        f.write('\n' + '=' * 55 + '\n')
        f.write('END OF REPORT\n')
    
    return report_name

# Create findings
findings = [
    {"title": "Brute force attack on SSH", "severity": "CRITICAL",
     "evidence": "203.0.113.42 — 500 failed logins in 5 minutes",
     "action": "Block IP, enable fail2ban"},
    {"title": "Unusual data transfer", "severity": "HIGH",
     "evidence": "15 GB uploaded to external IP at 3 AM",
     "action": "Investigate user account, check DLP logs"},
    {"title": "Outdated TLS version", "severity": "MEDIUM",
     "evidence": "Server accepting TLS 1.0 connections",
     "action": "Upgrade to TLS 1.3 minimum"},
]

filename = generate_report(findings)
print(f"Report saved: {filename}")

# Read back and display
with open(filename, 'r') as f:
    print(f.read())
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
This creates a PROFESSIONAL forensic report:
1. The function takes findings data and metadata parameters.
2. open(report_name, 'w') creates a new file.
3. f.write() builds the report section by section.
4. String formatting (f-strings, alignment) creates clean structure.
5. The function returns the filename so the caller knows where the report was saved.

In real forensic work, reports must be: structured, timestamped, attributed to an analyst, and reproducible. This template follows those requirements. Many forensic teams use Python to auto-generate reports from analysis data.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add a 'format' parameter that supports 'txt' and 'csv'. For CSV, output findings as a spreadsheet-style file.

complete_examples.title: Example 3: CSV Log Processor
complete_examples.description: Read, filter, and analyse structured CSV security data
complete_examples.code:
import csv
from io import StringIO
from collections import Counter

# Create sample CSV data (simulating a firewall export)
csv_data = """timestamp,source_ip,dest_ip,port,protocol,action,bytes
2025-02-28 08:00:01,203.0.113.42,10.0.0.5,22,TCP,BLOCK,0
2025-02-28 08:00:02,203.0.113.42,10.0.0.5,22,TCP,BLOCK,0
2025-02-28 08:00:03,203.0.113.42,10.0.0.5,22,TCP,BLOCK,0
2025-02-28 08:15:00,10.0.0.12,8.8.8.8,443,TCP,ALLOW,15234
2025-02-28 08:20:00,10.0.0.5,10.0.0.100,80,TCP,ALLOW,52100
2025-02-28 09:00:00,198.51.100.1,10.0.0.5,3306,TCP,BLOCK,0
2025-02-28 09:30:00,10.0.0.12,104.18.32.7,443,TCP,ALLOW,892034
2025-02-28 10:00:00,10.0.0.5,10.0.0.100,443,TCP,ALLOW,1024
2025-02-28 10:30:00,203.0.113.42,10.0.0.5,80,TCP,BLOCK,0
2025-02-28 11:00:00,172.16.0.99,10.0.0.5,4444,TCP,BLOCK,0
"""

# Write to file
with open('firewall.csv', 'w') as f:
    f.write(csv_data)

            blocked_ips[row['source_ip']] += 1
        else:

        port_targets[row['port']] += 1

print(f"Allowed:           {allowed}")

complete_examples.title: Example 4: Binary File Identifier (Magic Bytes)
complete_examples.description: Read file headers to identify true file types regardless of extension
complete_examples.code:
import os

# File signature database
FILE_SIGNATURES = {
    b'\xff\xd8\xff': ('JPEG', 'Image file'),
    b'\x89PNG\r\n\x1a\n': ('PNG', 'Image file'),
    b'GIF87a': ('GIF87', 'Image file'),
    b'GIF89a': ('GIF89', 'Animated image'),
    b'%PDF': ('PDF', 'Document'),
    b'PK\x03\x04': ('ZIP/DOCX/XLSX', 'Archive or Office doc'),
    b'MZ': ('EXE/DLL', 'Windows executable'),
    b'\x7fELF': ('ELF', 'Linux executable'),
    b'Rar!': ('RAR', 'Archive'),
    b'\x1f\x8b': ('GZIP', 'Compressed file'),
}

def identify_file_type(filepath):
    """Identify file type from magic bytes, not extension."""
    try:
        with open(filepath, 'rb') as f:
            header = f.read(16)
    except (FileNotFoundError, PermissionError) as e:
        return None, None, str(e)
    
    for signature, (name, desc) in FILE_SIGNATURES.items():
        if header[:len(signature)] == signature:
            return name, desc, None
    
    return 'UNKNOWN', f'Header: {header[:8].hex().upper()}', None

# Create test files with different signatures
test_files = {
    'photo.jpg': b'\xff\xd8\xff\xe0' + b'\x00' * 20,
    'document.pdf': b'%PDF-1.7' + b'\x00' * 20,
    'hidden.txt': b'MZ\x90\x00' + b'\x00' * 20,  # EXE disguised as .txt!
    'archive.zip': b'PK\x03\x04' + b'\x00' * 20,
    'mystery.dat': b'\x00\x01\x02\x03' + b'\x00' * 20,
}

for name, content in test_files.items():
    with open(name, 'wb') as f:
        f.write(content)

# Scan all files
print("FILE TYPE IDENTIFICATION")
print("=" * 60)
print(f"{'Filename':<18} {'Extension':<10} {'True Type':<15} {'Match?'}")
print("-" * 60)

alerts = []
for filename in test_files:
    ext = os.path.splitext(filename)[1]
    true_type, desc, error = identify_file_type(filename)
    
    # Check if extension matches true type
    ext_map = {'.jpg': 'JPEG', '.pdf': 'PDF', '.txt': 'UNKNOWN',
               '.zip': 'ZIP/DOCX/XLSX', '.dat': 'UNKNOWN'}
    expected = ext_map.get(ext, 'UNKNOWN')
    match = 'YES' if true_type == expected or expected == 'UNKNOWN' else 'MISMATCH!'
    
    if match == 'MISMATCH!':
        alerts.append(f"{filename}: claims to be {ext} but is actually {true_type}")
    
    print(f"{filename:<18} {ext:<10} {true_type:<15} {match}")

print("-" * 60)
if alerts:
    print(f"\nALERTS ({len(alerts)}):")
    for alert in alerts:
        print(f"  >>> {alert}")

------------------------------------------------------------
photo.jpg          .jpg       JPEG            YES
document.pdf       .pdf       PDF             YES
hidden.txt         .txt       EXE/DLL         MISMATCH!
archive.zip        .zip       ZIP/DOCX/XLSX   YES
mystery.dat        .dat       UNKNOWN         YES
------------------------------------------------------------

ALERTS (1):

complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Magic byte analysis is CRITICAL for forensics:
1. open(filepath, 'rb') reads raw bytes — not text.
2. f.read(16) gets the file header (first 16 bytes).
3. Comparison against known signatures identifies the TRUE file type.
4. Extension mismatch detection catches disguised files!

hidden.txt has extension .txt but its bytes start with MZ (Windows executable). An attacker renamed an .exe to .txt to hide it — magic bytes caught it!

This is how antivirus software and forensic tools like 'file' command on Linux work. Try on Kali: file hidden.txt — it will show 'PE32 executable' regardless of the .txt extension.

complete_examples.title: Example 5: File Hash Calculator & Verifier
complete_examples.description: Calculate and verify file hashes for evidence integrity
complete_examples.code:
import hashlib
import os

def hash_file(filepath, algorithm='sha256', chunk_size=4096):
    """Calculate hash of any file, regardless of size."""
    h = hashlib.new(algorithm)
    file_size = 0
    try:
        with open(filepath, 'rb') as f:
            while True:
                chunk = f.read(chunk_size)
                if not chunk:
                    break
                h.update(chunk)
                file_size += len(chunk)
    except FileNotFoundError:
        return None, 0
    return h.hexdigest(), file_size

def verify_integrity(filepath, expected_hash, algorithm='sha256'):
    """Verify a file has not been tampered with."""
    actual_hash, _ = hash_file(filepath, algorithm)
    return actual_hash == expected_hash

# Create test evidence files
files = {
    'evidence_001.txt': b'Critical forensic evidence - do not modify!',
    'evidence_002.txt': b'Financial records from suspect laptop',
    'evidence_003.txt': b'Chat logs extracted from mobile device',
}
for name, content in files.items():
    with open(name, 'wb') as f:
        f.write(content)

# Hash all evidence
print("EVIDENCE HASH MANIFEST")
print("=" * 70)
hash_manifest = {}
for filename in files:
    md5, _ = hash_file(filename, 'md5')
    sha256, size = hash_file(filename, 'sha256')
    hash_manifest[filename] = sha256
    print(f"\nFile: {filename} ({size} bytes)")
    print(f"  MD5:    {md5}")
    print(f"  SHA256: {sha256}")

# Tamper with one file
with open('evidence_002.txt', 'ab') as f:
    f.write(b' TAMPERED')  # Append data!

# Verify all files
print(f"\n{'=' * 70}")
print("INTEGRITY VERIFICATION")
print(f"{'=' * 70}")
for filename, expected in hash_manifest.items():
    is_valid = verify_integrity(filename, expected)
    status = 'VERIFIED' if is_valid else 'TAMPERED!'
    icon = '[OK]' if is_valid else '[!!]'
    print(f"  {icon} {filename}: {status}")
    if not is_valid:
        actual, _ = hash_file(filename)
        print(f"       Expected: {expected[:32]}...")
        print(f"       Got:      {actual[:32]}...")
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
File hashing is THE most critical forensic skill:
1. hash_file() reads in 4096-byte chunks — handles files of ANY size.
2. h.update(chunk) adds each chunk to the running hash.
3. The manifest records all hashes at the time of evidence seizure.
4. After simulated tampering (appending bytes), verification DETECTS the change.

In court, digital evidence must have documented chain of custody. Hash verification proves evidence was not modified between seizure and analysis. If hashes do not match, the evidence may be inadmissible.

This is exactly how tools like EnCase, FTK, and md5deep work. On Kali: sha256sum evidence_001.txt

import json
import datetime

def create_evidence_db(filepath):
    """Create a new evidence database."""
    db = {
        'metadata': {
            'case_id': 'FR-2025-042',
            'created': datetime.datetime.now().isoformat(),
            'analyst': 'Synapse Student'
        },
        'evidence': []
    }
    save_db(filepath, db)
    return db

def save_db(filepath, db):
    """Save database to JSON file."""
    with open(filepath, 'w') as f:
        json.dump(db, f, indent=2)

def load_db(filepath):
    """Load database from JSON file."""
    with open(filepath, 'r') as f:
        return json.load(f)

def add_evidence(db, name, etype, hash_val, notes=''):
    """Add an evidence item."""
    item = {
        'id': f'E{len(db["evidence"]) + 1:03d}',
        'name': name,
        'type': etype,
        'hash': hash_val,
        'notes': notes,
        'added': datetime.datetime.now().isoformat()
    }
    db['evidence'].append(item)
    return item

def search_evidence(db, query):
    """Search evidence by name or notes."""
    query = query.lower()
    return [e for e in db['evidence']
            if query in e['name'].lower() or query in e.get('notes', '').lower()]

# Build the database

print(f"\nSearch 'laptop': {len(results)} results")
for r in results:
    print(f"  {r['id']}: {r['name']} — {r['notes']}")

# Show the raw JSON

complete_examples.title: Example 7: Directory Scanner — Evidence Cataloguer
complete_examples.description: Recursively scan directories, classify files, and create an evidence catalogue
complete_examples.code:
import os
import hashlib

def quick_hash(filepath, size=1024):
    """Fast hash using only first 1024 bytes."""
    try:
        with open(filepath, 'rb') as f:
            return hashlib.md5(f.read(size)).hexdigest()[:12]
    except (PermissionError, OSError):
        return 'ERROR'

def classify_extension(ext):
    """Classify file by extension."""
    categories = {
        'image': ['.jpg', '.jpeg', '.png', '.gif', '.bmp', '.svg'],
        'document': ['.pdf', '.doc', '.docx', '.txt', '.md', '.csv'],
        'executable': ['.exe', '.bat', '.ps1', '.sh', '.py', '.cmd'],
        'archive': ['.zip', '.tar', '.gz', '.rar', '.7z'],
        'data': ['.json', '.xml', '.db', '.sql', '.log'],
    }
    for cat, exts in categories.items():
        if ext.lower() in exts:
            return cat
    return 'other'

def scan_directory(root_path):
    """Recursively scan a directory and catalogue all files."""
    catalogue = []
    
    for dirpath, dirnames, filenames in os.walk(root_path):
        for filename in filenames:
            filepath = os.path.join(dirpath, filename)
            ext = os.path.splitext(filename)[1]
            
            try:
                size = os.path.getsize(filepath)
            except OSError:
                size = 0
            
            catalogue.append({
                'path': filepath,
                'name': filename,
                'ext': ext,
                'category': classify_extension(ext),
                'size': size,
                'hash': quick_hash(filepath),
            })
    
    return catalogue

# Create test directory structure
os.makedirs('test_evidence/case_001/photos', exist_ok=True)
os.makedirs('test_evidence/case_001/logs', exist_ok=True)
os.makedirs('test_evidence/case_002', exist_ok=True)

test_files = [
    ('test_evidence/case_001/photos/img001.jpg', b'\xff\xd8\xff fake jpeg'),
    ('test_evidence/case_001/photos/img002.png', b'\x89PNG fake png'),
    ('test_evidence/case_001/logs/access.log', b'2025-02-28 GET /index.html'),
    ('test_evidence/case_001/logs/error.log', b'ERROR: connection refused'),
    ('test_evidence/case_001/report.pdf', b'%PDF-1.7 fake pdf'),
    ('test_evidence/case_002/malware.exe', b'MZ fake executable'),
    ('test_evidence/case_002/data.csv', b'ip,port,status\n10.0.0.1,80,open'),
    ('test_evidence/case_002/config.json', b'{"setting": "value"}'),
]
for path, content in test_files:
    with open(path, 'wb') as f:
        f.write(content)

# Scan!
catalogue = scan_directory('test_evidence')

# Report
print("EVIDENCE CATALOGUE")
print("=" * 65)
print(f"Total files: {len(catalogue)}")

# Group by category
from collections import Counter
cats = Counter(f['category'] for f in catalogue)
print(f"\nBy category:")
for cat, count in cats.most_common():
    print(f"  {cat:<12}: {count}")

print(f"\nFile listing:")
for f in sorted(catalogue, key=lambda x: x['path']):
    size_str = f"{f['size']:>6}"
    print(f"  [{f['category']:<10}] {f['name']:<20} {size_str}B  {f['hash']}")

# Cleanup
import shutil
shutil.rmtree('test_evidence')

By category:

  [data      ] config.json          20B  c3d4e5f6a7b8

os.walk() is the ULTIMATE file scanner:
1. os.walk(root) yields (dirpath, dirnames, filenames) for EVERY directory.
2. os.path.join(dirpath, filename) builds the full path safely.
3. Each file is classified by extension and hashed.

complete_examples.title: Example 8: Encoding Detective
complete_examples.description: Handle different file encodings and detect encoding issues
complete_examples.code:
# Encoding Detective — handle files from any system

def detect_encoding(filepath):
    """Try to detect file encoding by attempting multiple decodings."""
    encodings = ['utf-8', 'ascii', 'latin-1', 'utf-16', 'cp1252']
    
    with open(filepath, 'rb') as f:
        raw = f.read()
    
    results = []
    for enc in encodings:
        try:
            text = raw.decode(enc)
            # Check for common signs of wrong encoding
            garbled = sum(1 for c in text if ord(c) > 127 and c not in 'aeiou')
            results.append((enc, True, garbled, text[:50]))
        except (UnicodeDecodeError, UnicodeError):
            results.append((enc, False, -1, 'FAILED'))
    
    return results, raw

def safe_read(filepath):
    """Read file with automatic encoding detection."""
    for encoding in ['utf-8', 'latin-1']:
        try:
            with open(filepath, 'r', encoding=encoding) as f:
                content = f.read()
            return content, encoding
        except UnicodeDecodeError:
            continue
    with open(filepath, 'rb') as f:
        return f.read().decode('utf-8', errors='replace'), 'binary-fallback'

# Create files with different encodings
with open('utf8_file.txt', 'w', encoding='utf-8') as f:
    f.write('Hello World - UTF-8 encoded text')

with open('latin1_file.txt', 'wb') as f:
    f.write('Caf\xe9 cr\xe8me - Latin-1 text'.encode('latin-1'))

# Test safe reading
for filename in ['utf8_file.txt', 'latin1_file.txt']:
    content, encoding = safe_read(filename)
    print(f"{filename}:")
    print(f"  Encoding: {encoding}")
    print(f"  Content:  {content[:40]}")
    print(f"  Length:   {len(content)} chars")
    print()

# Show raw bytes
print("Byte-level analysis of latin1_file.txt:")
with open('latin1_file.txt', 'rb') as f:
    raw = f.read()
    for i, byte in enumerate(raw[:20]):
        char = chr(byte) if 32 <= byte < 127 else '.'
        print(f"  Byte {i:>2}: 0x{byte:02X} ({byte:>3}) '{char}'")

  Encoding: utf-8
  Content:  Hello World - UTF-8 encoded text
  Length:   32 chars

latin1_file.txt:
  Encoding: latin-1
  Content:  Cafe creme - Latin-1 text
  Length:   25 chars

Byte-level analysis of latin1_file.txt:
  Byte  0: 0x43 ( 67) 'C'
  Byte  1: 0x61 ( 97) 'a'
  Byte  2: 0x66 (102) 'f'
  Byte  3: 0xE9 (233) '.'  <- accented 'e'
  ...
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Encoding issues are common in forensics because evidence comes from diverse systems:
1. safe_read() tries UTF-8 first (most common), falls back to Latin-1 (accepts any byte).
2. Raw byte analysis reveals the actual encoding: 0xE9 is the Latin-1 code for accented 'e'.
3. errors='replace' prevents crashes by substituting unreadable characters.

When analysing evidence from Windows systems, you will often encounter CP-1252. From older servers, Latin-1. From international systems, various UTF variants. This defensive pattern handles them all.

On Kali: file --mime-encoding filename.txt shows the detected encoding.

import csv
import json
import datetime

def generate_reports(findings, case_id='FR-2025-001'):
    """Generate forensic reports in multiple formats."""
    timestamp = datetime.datetime.now().strftime('%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S')
    
    # TXT Report
    txt_file = f'{case_id}_report.txt'
    with open(txt_file, 'w') as f:
        f.write(f'FORENSIC REPORT - {case_id}\n')
        f.write(f'Generated: {timestamp}\n')
        f.write('=' * 50 + '\n\n')
        for i, finding in enumerate(findings, 1):
            f.write(f'[{i}] {finding["title"]}\n')
            f.write(f'    Severity: {finding["severity"]}\n')
            f.write(f'    Detail:   {finding["detail"]}\n\n')
    
    # CSV Report
    csv_file = f'{case_id}_report.csv'
    with open(csv_file, 'w', newline='') as f:
        writer = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=['id', 'title', 'severity', 'detail'])
        writer.writeheader()
        for i, finding in enumerate(findings, 1):
            writer.writerow({'id': i, **finding})
    
    # JSON Report
    json_file = f'{case_id}_report.json'
    report_data = {
        'case_id': case_id,
        'generated': timestamp,
        'total_findings': len(findings),
        'findings': findings
    }
    with open(json_file, 'w') as f:
        json.dump(report_data, f, indent=2)
    
    return [txt_file, csv_file, json_file]

# Findings data
findings = [
    {'title': 'SSH Brute Force', 'severity': 'CRITICAL', 'detail': '500 failed logins from 203.0.113.42'},
    {'title': 'Data Exfiltration', 'severity': 'HIGH', 'detail': '15 GB transfer to external IP'},
    {'title': 'Weak Password', 'severity': 'MEDIUM', 'detail': 'Admin account using default credentials'},
]

files = generate_reports(findings)
print("Reports generated:")
import os
for f in files:
    size = os.path.getsize(f)
    ext = os.path.splitext(f)[1]
    print(f"  {f:<30} {size:>6} bytes  ({ext})")

# Preview each
for f in files:
    print(f"\n--- {f} ---")
    with open(f, 'r') as fh:
        content = fh.read()
        # Show first 200 chars
        preview = content[:200] + ('...' if len(content) > 200 else '')
        print(preview)

complete_examples.code:
def compare_files(file1, file2, context_bytes=8):
    """Compare two files and report differences."""
    with open(file1, 'rb') as f1, open(file2, 'rb') as f2:
        data1 = f1.read()
        data2 = f2.read()
    
    print(f"File 1: {file1} ({len(data1)} bytes)")
    print(f"File 2: {file2} ({len(data2)} bytes)")
    print(f"Size match: {len(data1) == len(data2)}")
    
    # Find differences
    diffs = []
    min_len = min(len(data1), len(data2))
    for i in range(min_len):
        if data1[i] != data2[i]:
            diffs.append((i, data1[i], data2[i]))
    
    # Extra bytes
    if len(data1) != len(data2):
        extra_in = file1 if len(data1) > len(data2) else file2
        extra_bytes = abs(len(data1) - len(data2))
    else:
        extra_in = None
        extra_bytes = 0
    
    print(f"Differences: {len(diffs)} bytes differ")
    if extra_bytes:
        print(f"Extra bytes: {extra_bytes} in {extra_in}")
    
    if diffs:
        print(f"\nFirst 5 differences:")
        for offset, b1, b2 in diffs[:5]:
            c1 = chr(b1) if 32 <= b1 < 127 else '.'
            c2 = chr(b2) if 32 <= b2 < 127 else '.'
            print(f"  Offset 0x{offset:04X} ({offset:>5}): "
                  f"0x{b1:02X} '{c1}' vs 0x{b2:02X} '{c2}'")
    
    identical = len(diffs) == 0 and extra_bytes == 0
    return identical

# Create test files
original = b'The secret password is: forensics2025!\nKeep this safe.'
modified = b'The secret password is: CHANGED!!2025!\nKeep this safe.'

with open('original.bin', 'wb') as f: f.write(original)
with open('modified.bin', 'wb') as f: f.write(modified)

print("FILE COMPARISON REPORT")
print("=" * 50)
identical = compare_files('original.bin', 'modified.bin')
print(f"\nVerdict: {'IDENTICAL' if identical else 'FILES DIFFER — EVIDENCE MAY BE TAMPERED'}")

complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Byte-by-byte comparison is fundamental in forensics:
1. Both files opened in binary mode ('rb').
2. Each byte compared at the same offset.
3. Hex offsets show exactly WHERE changes occurred.
4. Character display shows WHAT changed (forensics→CHANGED).

This reveals not just THAT files differ, but exactly WHERE and HOW. In malware analysis, comparing a clean file to a suspected infected copy reveals the injected code.

On Kali: diff original.bin modified.bin or xxd original.bin | diff - <(xxd modified.bin)

complete_examples.title: Example 11: Streaming Log Monitor
complete_examples.description: Simulate tail -f: read new lines as they are appended to a log file
complete_examples.code:
import time
import os

def simulate_log_writes(filepath, entries):
    """Write log entries one at a time to simulate a live log."""
    with open(filepath, 'a') as f:
        for entry in entries:
            f.write(entry + '\n')
            f.flush()

def monitor_log(filepath, max_reads=10):
    """Monitor a log file for new entries (like tail -f)."""
    print(f"Monitoring: {filepath}")
    print("Watching for threats...\n")
    
    # Start from end of file
    if os.path.exists(filepath):
        with open(filepath, 'r') as f:
            f.seek(0, 2)  # Seek to end
            position = f.tell()
    else:
        position = 0
    
    threats = []
    reads = 0
    
    while reads < max_reads:
        with open(filepath, 'r') as f:
            f.seek(position)
            new_lines = f.readlines()
            position = f.tell()
        
        for line in new_lines:
            line = line.strip()
            if not line:
                continue
            reads += 1
            
            # Check for threats
            is_threat = any(w in line.upper() for w in ['FAILED', 'ATTACK', 'ERROR'])
            icon = '>>>' if is_threat else '   '
            print(f"{icon} {line}")
            
            if is_threat:
                threats.append(line)
        
        if not new_lines:
            break
    
    return threats

# Create initial empty log
with open('live.log', 'w') as f:
    f.write('')

# Simulate new entries being written
simulate_log_writes('live.log', [
    '2025-02-28 08:00:00 INFO Server started',
    '2025-02-28 08:15:00 WARNING Login FAILED user=admin',
    '2025-02-28 08:15:01 WARNING Login FAILED user=admin',
    '2025-02-28 08:30:00 INFO Backup complete',
    '2025-02-28 09:00:00 CRITICAL ATTACK detected from 203.0.113.42',
    '2025-02-28 09:30:00 INFO Normal operation resumed',
    '2025-02-28 10:00:00 ERROR Disk write failure',
])

# Monitor
threats = monitor_log('live.log')
print(f"\nTotal threats detected: {len(threats)}")
for t in threats:
    print(f"  ! {t}")

Monitoring: live.log
Watching for threats...

    2025-02-28 08:00:00 INFO Server started
>>> 2025-02-28 08:15:00 WARNING Login FAILED user=admin
>>> 2025-02-28 08:15:01 WARNING Login FAILED user=admin
    2025-02-28 08:30:00 INFO Backup complete
>>> 2025-02-28 09:00:00 CRITICAL ATTACK detected from 203.0.113.42
    2025-02-28 09:30:00 INFO Normal operation resumed
>>> 2025-02-28 10:00:00 ERROR Disk write failure

Total threats detected: 4
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
This simulates 'tail -f' — a core monitoring tool:
1. f.seek(0, 2) positions the cursor at the END of the file.
2. f.tell() saves the current position.
3. On each check, we seek to the saved position and read NEW lines only.
4. The position is updated after reading.

This is how real-time security monitoring works: SIEM systems continuously read new log entries as they appear, check for threat patterns, and generate alerts.

On Kali: tail -f /var/log/auth.log shows new authentication events in real-time. Your Python version adds custom threat detection logic!

complete_examples.title: Example 12: Complete Forensic File Processing Pipeline
complete_examples.description: End-to-end: scan, classify, hash, analyse, and report on an evidence directory
complete_examples.code:
import os, hashlib, json, csv, datetime

def process_evidence_directory(evidence_dir, output_dir):
    """Complete forensic processing pipeline."""
    # Ensure output directory exists
    os.makedirs(output_dir, exist_ok=True)
    
    # Phase 1: Catalogue
    print("Phase 1: Cataloguing files...")
    catalogue = []
    for dirpath, _, filenames in os.walk(evidence_dir):
        for fname in filenames:
            fpath = os.path.join(dirpath, fname)
            ext = os.path.splitext(fname)[1].lower()
            try:
                size = os.path.getsize(fpath)
                with open(fpath, 'rb') as f:
                    header = f.read(8)
                    f.seek(0)
                    sha = hashlib.sha256(f.read()).hexdigest()
            except OSError:
                continue
            
            # Classify risk
            risk = 'HIGH' if ext in ['.exe','.bat','.ps1'] else 'MEDIUM' if ext in ['.zip','.py'] else 'LOW'
            
            catalogue.append({
                'path': fpath, 'name': fname, 'ext': ext,
                'size': size, 'sha256': sha, 'risk': risk,
                'header_hex': header[:4].hex()
            })
    
    print(f"  Found {len(catalogue)} files")
    
    # Phase 2: Analysis
    print("Phase 2: Analysing...")
    stats = {
        'total_files': len(catalogue),
        'total_size': sum(f['size'] for f in catalogue),
        'by_risk': {}, 'by_ext': {},
        'duplicates': []
    }
    
    hash_seen = {}
    for item in catalogue:
        stats['by_risk'][item['risk']] = stats['by_risk'].get(item['risk'], 0) + 1
        stats['by_ext'][item['ext']] = stats['by_ext'].get(item['ext'], 0) + 1
        
        if item['sha256'] in hash_seen:
            stats['duplicates'].append((item['name'], hash_seen[item['sha256']]))
        else:
            hash_seen[item['sha256']] = item['name']
    
    # Phase 3: Generate reports
    print("Phase 3: Generating reports...")
    
    # CSV manifest
    csv_path = os.path.join(output_dir, 'manifest.csv')
    with open(csv_path, 'w', newline='') as f:
        w = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=['name','ext','size','sha256','risk','header_hex'])
        w.writeheader()
        for item in catalogue:
            w.writerow({k: item[k] for k in w.fieldnames})
    
    # JSON report
    json_path = os.path.join(output_dir, 'analysis.json')
    report = {
        'timestamp': datetime.datetime.now().isoformat(),
        'evidence_dir': evidence_dir,
        'stats': stats,
        'high_risk_files': [f for f in catalogue if f['risk'] == 'HIGH']
    }
    with open(json_path, 'w') as f:
        json.dump(report, f, indent=2)
    
    # Summary
    txt_path = os.path.join(output_dir, 'summary.txt')
    with open(txt_path, 'w') as f:
        f.write(f'Evidence Processing Summary\n{"=" * 40}\n')
        f.write(f'Files: {stats["total_files"]}\n')
        f.write(f'Size:  {stats["total_size"]:,} bytes\n')
        f.write(f'Risk:  {stats["by_risk"]}\n')
        f.write(f'Duplicates: {len(stats["duplicates"])}\n')
    
    return catalogue, stats

# Create test evidence
os.makedirs('evidence/suspect/docs', exist_ok=True)
for name, content in [('evidence/suspect/malware.exe', b'MZ evil'),
                       ('evidence/suspect/photo.jpg', b'\xff\xd8\xff image'),
                       ('evidence/suspect/docs/secret.pdf', b'%PDF secrets'),
                       ('evidence/suspect/docs/notes.txt', b'meeting notes'),
                       ('evidence/suspect/backup.zip', b'PK archive')]:
    with open(name, 'wb') as f: f.write(content)

catalogue, stats = process_evidence_directory('evidence', 'output')

print(f"\nRESULTS:")
print(f"  Files processed: {stats['total_files']}")
print(f"  Total size: {stats['total_size']:,} bytes")
print(f"  Risk breakdown: {stats['by_risk']}")
print(f"  Reports saved to: output/")

# Cleanup
import shutil
shutil.rmtree('evidence')
shutil.rmtree('output')
complete_examples.output:
Phase 1: Cataloguing files...
  Found 5 files
Phase 2: Analysing...
Phase 3: Generating reports...

RESULTS:
  Files processed: 5
  Total size: 42 bytes
  Risk breakdown: {'HIGH': 1, 'LOW': 3, 'MEDIUM': 1}
  Reports saved to: output/
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
This is a COMPLETE forensic processing pipeline:

Phase 1 (Catalogue): os.walk scans all files, reads headers, calculates hashes, classifies risk.
Phase 2 (Analysis): Statistics, risk breakdown, duplicate detection via hash comparison.
Phase 3 (Reports): CSV manifest, JSON analysis, TXT summary — three formats from one dataset.

This combines EVERY file I/O concept: reading text, writing reports, binary headers, CSV output, JSON export, directory walking, hashing, error handling, and path manipulation.

Professional forensic tools follow this exact pipeline: Ingest → Catalogue → Analyse → Report. You just built one from scratch!

os / os.path File paths, existence, size, walk csv Read/write comma-separated data json Read/write JSON data hashlib MD5, SHA-256 file hashing shutil Copy, move, delete files/dirs

hints.message: Hash once, append data, hash again, compare.

Solution:
import hashlib
def get_hash(path):
    with open(path, 'rb') as f:
        return hashlib.sha256(f.read()).hexdigest()

hash1 = get_hash('test.bin')
with open('test.bin', 'ab') as f:
    f.write(b' TAMPERED')
hash2 = get_hash('test.bin')
print(f'Match: {hash1 == hash2} {"(tampered!)" if hash1 != hash2 else ""}')
